justification: Chapter 4 inventory section 'Philo of Alexandria (lines 755-790)' covers John's Prologue Logos theology as crystallization of Alexandrian Jewish-Greek philosophy. Philo's Logos as instrument of creation, first-born son, image of God are all documented. Chapter 3 inventory also covers Logos as Greek philosophical theme (Heraclitus, Stoics, Middle Platonists, Philo). The finding's claim about Platonic/consciousness framework mapping onto John 1:1 is the same argument with the same evidence (John's Prologue + Platonic philosophy). Wallis's specific 'fractalizing consciousness' framing is New Age interpretation, not a new scholarly argument.

Chapter 4 inventory 'Identity of the Beloved Disciple (lines 94-151)' explicitly cites Gospel of Mary (Peter complains 'Did he really speak with a woman in private?', Coptic 17-18) and Gospel of Philip (63:32-36 'He loved her more than all the disciples'). Q&A documents extensive Gospel of Mary/Philip cross-textual love language matrix. The finding's evidence (Mary as sole recipient of inner knowledge, Peter's resistance, Levi defending her) is the same evidence already in the chapter and Q&A.

Chapter 4 inventory 'Mary Magdalene as Fourth Pillar (lines 481-497)' and 'Identity of the Beloved Disciple (lines 94-151)' establish Mary Magdalene's prominence alongside Peter. Q&A records 'Mary Magdalene's Statistical Prominence' section noting she is always named first in lists of women. The argument that Mary was a major leader whose authority was contested alongside Peter and Thomas is the same argument the chapter makes.

Chapter 4 inventory 'Textual Evidence of Women's Erasure (lines 455-479)' explicitly includes 'P66 and P75 show Mary/Martha names shifting positions in John 11-12' and 'John 11:27 confession may originally have been placed in mouth of Mary, not Martha.' Q&A documents 'Schrader-Polczer Martha/Mary Text-Critical Bombshell' in detail with the same P66 evidence and the same 'Mary split into two' argument.

The argument that Mary of Bethany and Mary Magdalene are the same person is implicit in the chapter's treatment (Q&A discusses Lazarus-as-BD and Mary/Martha text-critical evidence). However, the specific seven textual parallels between John 11 (Lazarus narrative) and John 20 (Mary Magdalene) — both at tombs, both cry, both encounter risen figure, both hear 'where have you laid him' — are not enumerated in the chapter or Q&A. These specific structural parallels are new evidence for the existing argument about Mary's unified identity.

The chapter argues Mary Magdalene was the Beloved Disciple and that scribes obscured female prominence. However, the specific evidence that Hippolytus and the Manichaeans identified Mary of Bethany with Mary Magdalene, and that fourth-century sarcophagi predominantly depict only one woman with Lazarus and Jesus, is not in the chapter inventory or Q&A. This is new archaeological/patristic evidence supporting the existing argument about Mary's unified identity.

Chapter 5 inventory explicitly covers 'The Gospel of John is the Alexandrian gospel, with Logos theology, Philo's philosophical vocabulary, and Egyptian ritual parallels' with detailed evidence including Alexandrian provenance. Chapter 4 inventory covers Philo's Logos theology in section 'Philo of Alexandria (lines 755-790)'. The argument that John originated in Alexandrian Greco-Egyptian theological context is already made with the same core evidence.

Chapter 4 inventory 'Textual Evidence of Women's Erasure (lines 455-479)' covers P66/P75 name-shifting in John 11-12 and the hypothesis that John 11:27 confession was originally Mary's. Q&A 'Schrader-Polczer Martha/Mary Text-Critical Bombshell' documents this exact research in detail. The finding adds no evidence beyond what is already documented.

The chapter inventory notes 'John 11:27 confession may originally have been placed in mouth of Mary, not Martha' but does not cite Tertullian's fourth-century commentary confirming that Mary (not Martha) gave this confession in earlier texts. Tertullian as a specific patristic witness is new evidence for the existing argument about scribal modification of female prominence.

The chapter argues Mary Magdalene is the Beloved Disciple and documents women at narrative hinges, but does not enumerate the seven specific textual parallels between the Lazarus narrative (John 11) and Mary Magdalene resurrection narrative (John 20). The count of seven identical parallels is new supporting evidence for the existing argument about the author's deliberate construction linking Mary across both narratives.

Chapter 4 inventory notes P66/P75 name-shifting but does not cite the statistical scale: Schrader's analysis of 250+ manuscripts showing 1-in-5 Greek manuscripts and 1-in-3 Old Latin manuscripts have alterations around Martha. This quantitative manuscript data is new evidence strengthening the existing argument about systematic editorial intervention to diminish female authority.

Chapter 4 inventory 'Textual Evidence of Women's Erasure (lines 455-479)' covers scribal modification of female prominence in John, and Q&A documents Schrader-Polczer research. The finding's general claim about Mary's involvement varying across manuscripts at crucifixion and resurrection is the same argument already present.

Chapter 4 inventory 'Textual Evidence of Women's Erasure (lines 455-479)' includes 'John 11:27 confession may originally have been placed in mouth of Mary, not Martha.' Q&A 'Schrader-Polczer Martha/Mary Text-Critical Bombshell' documents the same hypothesis that Martha was added to dilute Mary Magdalene's prominence.

The chapter argues for female authorship of John and Q&A documents 15 female authorship tests including references to Sappho and Oxyrhynchus papyri women. However, Perpetua's diary (200-203 CE) as a specific named example of a wealthy educated woman whose rhetorical work survived is not mentioned in either the chapter inventory or Q&A. This is new specific evidence for the existing argument about the plausibility of female authorship in antiquity.

Chapter 4 inventory 'Identity of the Beloved Disciple (lines 94-151)' explicitly cites Gospel of Philip (63:32-36 'He loved her more than all the disciples') and Gospel of Mary (Coptic 17-18). Q&A documents the cross-textual love language matrix from these exact texts.

Martin asserts John was not written by an eyewitness but provides no argument — just states the mainstream consensus position. The book already addresses and argues against this position extensively in ch4 (eyewitness evidence sections, 15 female authorship tests, domestic-interior voice analysis). A bare assertion without supporting evidence is not a contradiction — it is the position the book already engages with and rebuts.

Chapter 4 inventory section 'Introduction (lines 13-31)' states 'Gospels certainly had many editions (John likely 3rd edition).' The sedimented layers approach (Raymond Brown, Lou Martin) documenting christological development across different periods is the same multi-edition argument already in the chapter.

The chapter argues for John's early composition and institutional context but does not specifically discuss the aposunagogos (expelled from synagogue) term appearing three times in John, or how this institutional rupture produced higher christology. The specific Greek term and its triple occurrence as evidence of community conflict shaping theology is new evidence for the existing argument about John's institutional context.

Chapter 4 inventory 'Identity of the Beloved Disciple (lines 94-151)' documents women staying while male apostles fled (Mark 15:40-41), Mary Magdalene at the cross (John 19:25), and Gospel of Mary/Philip/Pistis Sophia establishing Mary's primacy. The finding's general claim about women in Jesus's inner circle is already covered.

Chapter 4 inventory 'Philo of Alexandria (lines 755-790)' extensively documents John's Logos theology as crystallization of Alexandrian Jewish-Greek philosophy. Chapter 3 covers the Logos concept from Heraclitus through Stoics to Philo. The finding's claim that Johannine Christology is a product of Greco-Christian intellectual frameworks is the same argument with the same evidence.

Chapter 4 inventory 'Philo of Alexandria (lines 755-790)' documents that Philo's Logos theology constituted 'high Christology' before Jesus, making John's Prologue a crystallization of existing theology. The 'Johannine Prologue's Logos theology' section (lines 792-797) also covers this. The finding adds no new evidence beyond what is already documented.

Chapter 4 inventory 'Baptism as coronation (lines 1014-1098)' documents John's distinctive treatment of baptism including 'Gospel of John never uses metanoia or metanoeo.' The finding's observation that 'I did not know him' appears only in John and that John is distinctive is already covered by the chapter's analysis of John's unique baptism presentation.

Chapter 4 inventory 'Identity of the Beloved Disciple (lines 94-151)' explicitly cites Gospel of Philip (63:32-36): 'He loved her more than all the disciples.' Q&A's cross-textual love language matrix documents the Gospel of Philip's claim about Mary Magdalene. The consort/wife claim is the same text.

Chapter 4 inventory 'Identity of the Beloved Disciple (lines 94-151)' cites Gospel of Mary (Coptic 17-18) with Peter's complaint. Q&A documents Pistis Sophia where 'Peter complains she won't stop talking' and the three independent texts all preserving Peter-Mary conflict. Finding is the same evidence.

Chapter 4 inventory cites Gospel of Mary and Gospel of Philip on Mary's special knowledge. Q&A documents the cross-textual matrix. The finding's reference to Gospels of Mary and Thomas showing Mary as companion with greater knowledge, threatening Peter, is the same evidence.

Chapter 4 inventory 'John leaves out the Eucharist (lines 678-705)' mentions Clement of Alexandria comparing Jesus with Dionysus and Cana water-to-wine reproducing Dionysus's signature act. However, the finding adds a specific new piece: McDonald's identification of parallels between John and Dionysus mythology that goes beyond water-to-wine to include the Samaritan woman scene as parallel to provision of water. The Samaritan woman as Dionysian parallel is not in the chapter.

Chapter 4 inventory 'Identity of the Beloved Disciple (lines 94-151)' documents Peter-Mary rivalry from Gospel of Mary, Gospel of Philip, and Pistis Sophia. Q&A records the cross-textual pattern. The finding's description of Peter-Mary conflict about priority is the same argument and evidence.

The chapter documents women's erasure in textual transmission (Junia, P66/P75 name shifts) and Mary Magdalene's prominence. However, the specific claim that 'by the time you have the people collecting the books of the New Testament any book that elevated the role of women wasn't going to be included' — the canon-formation mechanism of exclusion — is not explicitly stated in the chapter inventory. This is new evidence (canon-level exclusion) for the existing argument about systematic suppression of female authority.

Chapter 4 Q&A notes 'In Egypt, women wrote a lot' and discusses female education among upper-class women. The chapter inventory does not include the specific literacy rate (10-15%) as a data point. However, the argument about female authorship plausibility is already made. The literacy rate statistic is new quantitative evidence for the existing argument about the significance of the authorship claim.

Chapter 4 inventory 'Hostile witnesses / Polycarp / Paul / Philo (lines 737-790)' mentions Polycarp/Irenaeus chain but does not cite Eusebius's account via Clement about John writing at students' request, nor the explicit parallel to Pythagorean, Platonic, and Stoic schools recording teachers' teachings. This Greco-philosophical school parallel for John's composition is new evidence for the existing argument about John's Greco-Christian institutional context.

Chapter 4 inventory documents John's sophisticated Greek composition throughout, including Logos theology, Philo parallels, and the genre matching Greco-Roman bios. The chapter's Philo section and genre section establish exactly this: a highly trained Greek author. Chapter 5 explicitly states John is 'the Alexandrian gospel.'

The chapter establishes John's institutional context and early dating but does not specifically frame the Johannine community as Jewish believers expelled from synagogue who produced 'in-house literature.' The aposunagogos concept as institutional context for John's higher christology is new framing for the existing argument. See also finding cjCRhgD_XIQ_4 which covers related material.

Chapter 4 inventory 'Dating of the Gospel of John (lines 72-92)' explicitly states 'John 21 is by a different author, an appendix; John 1-20 ends naturally at 20:30-31.' The finding covers the same evidence: Chapter 21 as later addition and Prologue as possibly different theology from the rest.

Chapter 4 inventory 'Philo of Alexandria (lines 755-790)' and Chapter 3 inventory on Logos document Stoic and Platonic philosophical influence on John. The finding's general claim about Stoic and Platonic influence throughout John is the same argument.

Chapter 4 inventory cites Gospel of Mary (Coptic 17-18) on Mary as recipient of special knowledge. Q&A documents this extensively. The finding's claim about the Gospel of Mary as possibly female-authored and establishing Mary as 'true heir of apostolic Christianity' is covered by the existing discussion.

Chapter 4 inventory 'Archaeological evidence confirms John's detailed pre-70 CE Jerusalem topography' (lines 106-117) and the overall argument for John's eyewitness reliability directly present the case for John's historical value. The finding's claim about rehabilitation of John is the same argument the chapter makes.

Chapter 4 inventory 'Textual Evidence of Women's Erasure (lines 455-479)' and 'Mary Magdalene as Fourth Pillar (lines 481-497)' cover systematic marginalization of Mary Magdalene's role. The finding's general claim that 'influential women written out of the story' is the same argument.

Chapter 4 inventory covers John's Logos theology extensively (Philo section), and the chapter notes John never uses metanoia/metanoeo. Chapter 5 inventory explicitly argues 'John is not a Jewish text.' The finding's observation about logos/eternal pre-existence and absence of baptism narrative is covered.

This finding about 6th-century Syrian Christian authors adapting Ishtar's lamentations over Tammuz into Virgin Mary mourning narratives is relevant to Chapter 3 (Greco-Christian framework, Son of God titles, parallel theological structures) or Chapter 6 (institutional continuity, cultural exchange between traditions). It does not address Chapter 4's focus on Gospel reliability, Johannine authorship, or dating.

Chapter 4 inventory section 'Introduction (lines 13-31)' notes 'Gospels certainly had many editions' and the discussion of what John includes/excludes. The chapter's treatment of John's baptism section notes John has no birth narrative implicitly. The Q&A global decision on 'Johannine female authorship' discusses John's distinctive features. The absence of nativity/virgin birth in John as supporting Johannine primacy is already part of the argument.

Chapter 4 inventory 'Identity of the Beloved Disciple (lines 94-151)' cites Gospel of Mary and three independent texts (Gospel of Mary, Gospel of Philip, Pistis Sophia) on Mary's special status. Q&A documents the cross-textual matrix extensively. The 'apostle to the apostles' designation is part of the established argument.

The chapter argues for female authorship plausibility. Q&A documents 15 tests including women writing in Egypt. However, Marcelina appealing to Mary as an apostolic figure, and the Gospel of Mary author as 'someone who takes female apostleship and authority seriously' in second-century context, while partially covered, the specific detail about Marcelina using Mary as apostolic authority is not explicitly in the chapter inventory. This adds a named historical example.

Chapter 4 inventory 'Philo of Alexandria (lines 755-790)' covers John's use of Philo's theology, and the 'Hostile witnesses / Polycarp / Paul / Philo' section documents the progression. Chapter 3 covers the Logos concept development. The progressive christological development from Mark to John is the standard scholarly framework already embedded in the chapter.

Chapter 4 discusses John's resurrection narrative and Greco-Roman literary conventions, but does not cite Apollonius of Tyana's appearance to disciples saying 'touch me, you'll see I am not a ghost' as a specific parallel to Johannine resurrection appearances. This is new comparative evidence (Apollonius) for the existing argument about John operating within Greco-Roman theological conventions.

The chapter documents John's centrality to Hellenistic Christianity but does not mention the Carpocratians' exclusive use of John or their theology of baptism as mechanism of divine transformation. This is new evidence about a specific early Christian sect's relationship with John, supporting the existing argument about John's special status in Hellenistic communities.

The chapter documents John's Greco-Christian framework but does not specifically frame Jesus as 'fulfilling the mysteries in Athens and Anatolia' or use the language of mystery religion fulfillment across specific geographic cult centers. The mystery-religion geographic fulfillment framing (Eleusis, Athens, Anatolia) is a new specific angle for the existing argument about John's Greco-Christian orientation.

Chapter 4 inventory 'John leaves out the Eucharist (lines 678-705)' documents Cana water-to-wine as Dionysus's signature act and Clement of Alexandria engaging the Jesus-Dionysus parallel. The finding's general claim about John-Dionysus parallels in mythic and philosophical space is already covered.

Chapter 4 inventory 'Archaeological evidence confirms John's detailed pre-70 CE Jerusalem topography (lines 106-117)' explicitly lists Pool of Bethesda, Pool of Siloam, Caiaphas family tomb, Kidron valley, Gabbatha, Jacob's well. The finding cites the same archaeological sites (Pool of Bethesda, Pool of Siloam, Pilate's judgment seat) and the same argument.

The chapter covers John's Greco-Roman bios genre and Luke's historiographical conventions. However, Walsh's specific framework categorizing Mark as mystery novel, Matthew as stoic text/sacred scripture, and Luke as Greek novel is not in the chapter. The systematic genre-differentiation across all Synoptics by a named scholar is new evidence for the existing argument about Gospel authors' Greco-Roman literary sophistication.

Chapter 4 inventory 'Philo of Alexandria (lines 755-790)' and Chapter 3 inventory on Logos document Platonic philosophical influence on John. The finding's general claim about Platonic overheads and the logos with a triad is the same argument.

Chapter 4 inventory 'John leaves out the Eucharist (lines 678-705)' documents Jesus-Dionysus parallels in John. The finding's framing of Jesus as 'philosophical cynic stoic type of thinker' who is 'the Bacchus philosopher that you get in the Platonist' is the same Dionysian-philosophical argument.

The chapter documents John's eyewitness authority and primacy, but does not cite early church fathers rejecting Infancy Gospel of Thomas childhood miracles specifically because John's Cana narrative says 'this is Jesus's first sign.' This is new evidence of John's narrative authority being used as the standard by early church fathers, supporting the existing Johannine primacy argument.

Chapter 4 inventory 'Identity of the Beloved Disciple (lines 94-151)' documents the Peter-Mary rivalry across multiple texts. Q&A discusses the 'egalitarian proto-feminist Christianity' vs 'patriarchal Catholicism' dynamic already through the Gospel of Mary. The finding restates the same argument with the same texts.

Chapter 4 inventory notes 'John likely 3rd edition' but does not name McDonald's specific three-stage model (Dionysian Gospel -> Anti-Jewish Gospel -> Beloved Disciple Gospel). The argument that John had multiple editions exists, but the specific Dionysian-first compositional sequence is new evidence for the existing argument about John's Hellenistic foundations.

This finding about mystery religion structures (outer parables for masses, inner mysteries for initiates) and Gospel of Thomas's secrecy motif pertains to the broader institutional framework of early Christianity. It is more relevant to Chapter 5 (Pauline Epistles, ecclesial structure) or Chapter 3 (Greco-Christian institutional framework) than to Chapter 4's focus on Gospel reliability and Johannine authorship.

Chapter 4 inventory 'John leaves out the Eucharist (lines 678-705)' documents Cana water-to-wine as Dionysus's act. The finding's claim about Dionysian cultic practices relating to John 2 and John being intended for Greco-Roman audience is already covered.

Chapter 4 inventory 'John leaves out the Eucharist (lines 678-705)' documents John's foot-washing replacing bread/wine. However, the specific argument about John deliberately changing the crucifixion date to Passover eve (14th Nisan instead of 15th) to align with lamb theology, and the Egyptian theology connections (Book of the Dead, Osiris, mystery religion practices) are not in the chapter inventory. The date-change mechanism and Egyptian theological parallels are new evidence for the existing argument about John's distinctive theological framework.

The chapter does not argue that Dionysian female devotees (Maenads) experiencing mystical communion with the god provide a framework for understanding Mary Magdalene's role in early Christian mystical theology. Neither the Maenad parallel nor the mystical-erotic-union-with-deity framework for understanding female figures in early Christianity appears in the chapter or Q&A. This is both a new argument (Maenad-as-framework-for-Mary) and new evidence (specific ancient source descriptions of Maenad experiences).

Chapter 4 inventory 'John leaves out the Eucharist (lines 678-705)' documents Jesus-Dionysus parallels, and the argument about Greco-Roman literary framework is throughout the chapter. The finding's claim about John's narrative structure paralleling Euripides' Bacchae is the same Dionysian argument already covered.

Chapter 4 inventory 'Identity of the Beloved Disciple (lines 94-151)' cites Gospel of Mary (Coptic 17-18) with the same Peter-jealousy and Mary-secret-teaching pattern. Q&A documents this extensively.

Chapter 4 inventory 'Baptism as coronation (lines 1014-1098)' extensively documents baptism as coronation/investiture rather than repentance, with Psalm 2:7, divine proclamation, and the three-part coronation sequence. The finding's framing of John's baptism as Epiphany/divine manifestation is the same argument.

Chapter 4 inventory 'John leaves out the Eucharist (lines 678-705)' documents Cana water-to-wine as Dionysus's signature act. However, the specific evidence that five pre-gospel Dionysian water-to-wine miracles existed (with primary source references), and that the ceremonial jugs at Cana match priestly vessels used in Dionysian epiphany rites, is not enumerated in the chapter inventory. These are new specific primary source parallels for the existing Dionysian argument.

The chapter covers Philo's Logos theology but does not discuss Philo's allegorical interpretation of Miriam/Mary as 'perception/aesis' and Aaron as 'logos/reason.' The argument that Philo creates a direct philosophical genealogy for the Mary figure in early Christianity, linking it to Platonic epistemology and Egyptian Isis imagery, is neither in the chapter inventory nor Q&A. Both the argument (Mary-figure as Platonic philosophical category via Philo) and the evidence (Philo's Allegoriae of the Law passage) are new.

Neither the chapter inventory nor Q&A discusses the etymological connection between the name Mary/Miriam and Egyptian Isis, or the 4th-century BC source calling Isis 'Mary Isis.' The argument that the Mary figure connects to Isis through name and function, and Philo's Miriam aligns with Dionysian/Bacchic priestess imagery, is a new argument with new evidence not present in the book.

Chapter 4 inventory 'Argument from Bodily Silence (lines 614-677)' documents the Beloved Disciple relationship and explicitly argues against homoerotic male authorship. The chapter addresses the Greek audience context and the Beloved Disciple relationship. The finding covers the same territory.

Chapter 4 inventory 'Philo of Alexandria (lines 755-790)' and 'Christos vs Messiah (lines 526-581)' document John's distinctive high Christology and its Greco-Christian context. The finding's claim about John presenting Jesus as 'fully God' vs other gospels' 'Son of God' is the same argument.

Chapter 4 inventory discusses the Beloved Disciple reclining in Jesus's bosom (lines 614-677) but does not specifically document translation bias — how translation committees actively tone down the physical proximity language in John 13. This is new evidence (translation-level bias) for the existing argument about textual transmission and the Beloved Disciple's characterization.

Chapter 4 covers John's Logos theology through Philo but does not discuss the Zoe (life) concept as a distinct theological category, nor how Gnostic and Valentinian schools interpreted John through Greek philosophical categories. The Logos-Zoe paired concept as used by specific Hellenistic Christian sects is new evidence for the existing Greco-Christian framework argument.

Chapter 4 inventory 'Philo of Alexandria (lines 755-790)' and Chapter 3 inventory on Logos cover the philosophical lineage (Heraclitus, Stoics, Plato, Philo). The finding adds Pythagoras and Plutarch to the chain but the argument is the same.

Chapter 3 and 4 cover Logos from Heraclitus/Stoics/Philo, but the specific Pythagorean monad-to-diad-to-triad mathematical model as the origin of Trinitarian theology is not in the chapter inventories. The Pythagorean numerical-philosophical framework is new evidence for the existing Logos/Greek philosophy argument.

Chapter 4 cites Gospel of Mary, Gospel of Philip, and Pistis Sophia, but does not mention the 'Greater Questions of Mary' (cited by Epiphanius) showing Mary Magdalene in direct dialogue with Jesus on a mountain. This is a new specific apocryphal text supporting the existing argument about Mary Magdalene's prominence.

Chapter 4 documents the Beloved Disciple's identity but does not cite the Secret Book of John's explicit statement that John was 'loved more because he knew more.' This is new textual evidence linking special knowledge to the 'beloved' designation, supporting the existing argument about the Beloved Disciple's epistemological primacy.

Chapter 4 Q&A discusses female authorship plausibility (Test 9: women wrote in Egypt). The chapter does not mention Marcelina establishing 'the only known Roman Christian group in the second century to have been led entirely by a woman.' This is new specific historical evidence for the existing argument about female religious authority in early Christianity.

Chapter 4 Q&A discusses female authorship tests including Jewish midrashic precedent (Test 13) but does not cite Greco-Roman female prophetic authority (Pythia, Sibyls, oracular priestesses) as background evidence. The Greco-Roman female religious authority tradition is new evidence for the existing argument about the plausibility of female authorship and leadership.

Chapter 4 inventory 'John leaves out the Eucharist (lines 678-705)' documents Cana-Dionysus parallels. The finding repeats the same argument about Dionysian parallels in John's water-to-wine narrative and John's gentile audience. Bultmann's specific attribution does not add new evidence beyond what is already argued.

The chapter documents female authority in early Christianity but does not cite Marcus the Valentinian's specific practice of having women officiate at eucharist and deliver prophetic sermons. This is new primary source evidence for the existing argument about female liturgical authority in early Christianity.

Chapter 4 inventory 'Philo of Alexandria (lines 755-790)' extensively documents the John-Philo connection, listing scholarly support including Attridge by name. The finding covers the same connection.

Chapter 4 documents John-Dionysus parallels in the Eucharist/Cana sections but does not specifically cite MacDonald's argument that the Logos prologue imitates Dionysus's opening monologue in the Bacchae, or that the woman at the well parallels the Maenads. These are new specific literary parallels (prologue and Samaritan woman scenes) for the existing Dionysian argument.

Chapter 4 discusses John's primacy and Greek philosophical framework but does not specifically document that Sethians, Valentinians, Carpocratians, and Simonians all favored John over synoptics and shared the Logos-Zoe trinitarian doctrine. The systematic preference of multiple named Gnostic sects for John is new evidence for the existing Johannine primacy argument.

Chapter 4 documents Mary Magdalene and Junia. The finding adds Marcelina, Priscilla, and Maxilla as named women holding significant leadership roles in early Christianity. These additional named figures are new evidence for the existing argument about institutionalized female religious authority.
Same as k4UpbfDWPh8_0. The Pythagorean monad/diad/triad model as specifically paralleled in John's Logos/Zoe structure is not in the chapter inventory. New evidence for the existing Logos argument.

Neither the chapter inventory nor Q&A discusses the specific calendrical replacement of Isis's July 22 feast day with Mary Magdalene's feast day by the 4th-century Catholic and Orthodox church. This is both a new argument (deliberate calendrical Christianization linking Isis to Mary Magdalene) and new evidence (the specific date correspondence) not present in the book.

Chapter 4 inventory 'Philo of Alexandria (lines 755-790)' and Chapter 3 Logos section cover Middle Platonic theological framework in John. The finding restates the same argument about Middle Platonic Logos theology.

Chapter 4 inventory section 'Gospel of Marcion (lines 1278-1309)' documents Marcion's gospel opening at Luke 3:1 with no infancy narratives, supporting the claim that Luke's nativity was a secondary addition. The finding restates the same argument.

Chapter 4 documents John's sophisticated Greek composition and genre matching Greco-Roman bios. However, Walsh's specific thesis from 'The Origins of Early Christian Literature' — that ALL gospels were written by trained literary experts for literary audiences rather than communities recording oral tradition — is not cited in the chapter. Walsh's specific thesis is new evidence for the existing argument about sophisticated Gospel authorship.

Chapter 4 inventory 'Gospel of Matthew (lines 1266-1270)' briefly covers Matthew authorship but does not include the specific evidence that Matthew depends on a Greek mistranslation of Hebrew (the virgin birth prophecy from LXX Isaiah 7:14 using parthenos), proving original Greek composition. This is new evidence for the existing argument about Greek foundations of Gospel composition.

Chapter 4 inventory 'Identity of the Beloved Disciple (lines 94-151)' documents that the Beloved Disciple was NOT one of the Twelve and explicitly names John among those the Gospel distinguishes from the BD. The chapter's entire argument for alternative authorship (Mary Magdalene) rests on the premise that John son of Zebedee was not the author. The finding restates this prerequisite.

Chapter 4 inventory 'Philo of Alexandria (lines 755-790)' and 'Johannine Prologue's Logos theology (lines 792-797)' document John as philosophically sophisticated and emerging from Greco-Roman Jewish intellectual context. The finding restates the same argument.

Chapter 4 inventory 'Philo of Alexandria (lines 755-790)' documents the John-Philo Logos connection in detail, including Philo's philosophical synthesis bridging Jewish and Greek traditions. The finding covers the same territory.

Chapter 4 inventory 'Textual Evidence of Women's Erasure (lines 455-479)' explicitly includes 'Romans 16:7: Junia — grammatically/historically female, masculinized by later scribes; no attested male form Junias exists.' The finding presents the same evidence.

Chapter 4 inventory 'Textual Evidence of Women's Erasure (lines 455-479)' covers P66/P75 name-shifting and the hypothesis about Martha being added. Q&A documents Schrader-Polczer research in detail. Same evidence and argument.

Chapter 4 inventory 'Mary Magdalene as Fourth Pillar (lines 481-497)' and 'Textual Evidence of Women's Erasure (lines 455-479)' document exactly this: Mary's significance and systematic attempts to obscure it. Same argument.
Chapter 4 inventory 'Textual Evidence of Women's Erasure (lines 455-479)' explicitly cites Romans 16:7 Junia case. Same evidence.

Chapter 4 extensively documents John's Greco-Roman literary and philosophical embeddedness throughout multiple sections (Philo, Logos, genre, archaeological evidence, Christos vs Messiah). The finding's general claim is already the chapter's central thesis.

Chapter 4 documents John's Christological claims but does not specifically analyze the ego eimi mechanism as assertion of Tetragrammaton possession giving authorized divine power, framed within Hellenistic mediator-figure conventions. The specific theological mechanism of the 'I am' sayings as divine name-possession rhetoric is new evidence for the existing argument about John's Christology.
Chapter 4 inventory 'Philo of Alexandria (lines 755-790)' extensively documents this exact connection with multiple specific Philo citations.

Chapter 4 argues the Beloved Disciple is not John son of Zebedee and documents late authorship attributions (Irenaeus c.180 CE). However, the specific manuscript evidence that 'names and titles get moved around, found in different parts of manuscripts' — demonstrating they are scribal additions rather than original — is not enumerated in the chapter inventory. This is new textual transmission evidence for the existing argument.

Chapter 4 inventory 'Dating of the Gospel of John (lines 34-70)' states 'Irenaeus attests Mark/Matthew/Luke only in 180 AD.' The finding covers the same evidence about late attribution and secondary editorial nature of Gospel titles.

Chapter 4 inventory mentions 'Jewish Wisdom tradition: Sophia speaking in first person (Proverbs 8, Wisdom of Solomon), Philo's Logos bridges Jewish Wisdom and Greek philosophy.' However, the specific argument that Jesus = Logos = Sophia (divine wisdom personified as goddess) is not fully developed in the chapter. The explicit Sophia-Logos identification through which Jesus becomes the personified goddess of wisdom is new framing for the existing Logos evidence.

Chapter 4 documents Junia and textual erasure of women. The finding adds archaeological evidence that women held ecclesiastical positions through the 4th-6th century CE, and that manuscripts only began masculinizing Junia's name 'about a thousand years after Romans was written.' The archaeological data and the millennium-long timeline for name masculinization are new evidence for the existing argument.

The chapter argues for understanding John in its original context. The finding's specific point — that scholars avoid rendering 'the word was divine' because 'after john was written that would become a heresy' and therefore retroject later doctrinal categories — is a new specific example of scholarly bias not documented in the chapter inventory.

Chapter 4 inventory 'Hostile witnesses / Polycarp / Paul / Philo (lines 737-790)' documents the ontological relationship of Jesus to God already present by John's composition. The finding's reference to Bird's work confirming this is the same argument. No new evidence beyond what the chapter already establishes.
Chapter 4 inventory 'Philo of Alexandria (lines 755-790)' covers exactly this: John's Logos as emanation from God, paralleling Philo's framework. Same argument, same evidence.

Chapter 4 inventory 'Johannine Prologue's Logos theology (lines 792-797)' and 'Philo of Alexandria' section document John's Logos theology. Chapter 5 notes 'John's opening Logos matches Philo's De Opificio Mundi.' The Septuagint-mirroring point is covered.

Chapter 4 documents John's Greek philosophical framework but does not specifically argue that the concept of spirit as immaterial (vs. Hebrew/ancient understanding of spirit as material) was introduced by Greek philosophy and appears specifically in John and Paul as the most Greek-philosophically-educated NT authors. This is a new specific philosophical-linguistic argument for the existing Greco-Christian framework thesis.

Chapter 4 inventory 'Mary Magdalene as Fourth Pillar (lines 481-497)' states 'Latin West reduced her to penitent ex-prostitute (different from Eastern memory).' The finding covers the same correction, adding the specific attribution to Pope Gregory the Great in 591 CE, but the core argument is the same.

Chapter 4 inventory 'Identity of the Beloved Disciple (lines 94-151)' documents that all gospels indicate male apostles fled and only women stayed, and the chapter's female authorship argument relies on women's presence as witnesses. The criterion of embarrassment argument about female witnesses is implied in the chapter's treatment. Q&A notes 'Mary Magdalene's Statistical Prominence' and women's eyewitness role.

Chapter 4 inventory 'Introduction (lines 13-31)' states 'John likely 3rd edition' and 'Dating of the Gospel of John (lines 72-92)' notes John 21 is by a different author. The finding about multiple layers and displaced chapters is the same argument.

Chapter 4 extensively documents John as written for Greek audience and discusses the Beloved Disciple within Greek cultural conventions (lines 614-677 on bodily silence, Greek lover-beloved conventions). Chapter 5 argues 'John is not a Jewish text.' The finding restates covered arguments.

Chapter 4 notes John's multiple editions but does not name MacDonald's specific 'Dionysian Gospel' as the first compositional layer transitioning to 'anti-Jewish gospel' then 'gospel of the beloved disciple.' This specific three-stage compositional model is new evidence for the existing argument about John's Hellenistic foundations and editorial history.
Chapter 4 inventory cites Gospel of Mary, and Q&A documents Mary's role extensively including 'apostle to the apostles.' The finding restates the same evidence from the same text.

Chapter 4 inventory 'John leaves out the Eucharist (lines 678-705)' documents foot-washing in John 13 but does not cite the Homeric parallel to Eurycleia washing Odysseus's feet (with the name meaning 'far and wide' connecting to Jesus's promise about the woman's story). This specific Homeric literary parallel is new evidence for the existing argument about Greek literary conventions in the Gospels.

Chapter 4 inventory 'Argument from Bodily Silence (lines 614-677)' addresses the Greek cultural reading of the Beloved Disciple, including ancient homoerotic male literature conventions. The finding restates the same territory. The chapter specifically argues AGAINST the homoerotic reading by demonstrating absence of physical/beauty language.

Chapter 4 documents John-Dionysus parallels extensively. The finding adds Whitmarsh discussing MacDonald's work on the same parallels (Cana water-to-wine as 'the Dionysiac act'). Same argument and evidence.

Chapter 4 inventory 'Identity of the Beloved Disciple (lines 94-151)' documents Peter-Mary rivalry from Gospel of Mary and other texts. Q&A documents the cross-textual love language matrix. The finding restates the same pattern.
Q&A documents Gospel of Mary extensively, including Mary teaching the disciples and providing theological leadership. The finding restates the same evidence.

Chapter 4 inventory 'Textual Evidence of Women's Erasure (lines 455-479)' and 'Mary Magdalene as Fourth Pillar (lines 481-497)' document systematic suppression of female authority. The finding adds Pagels as a source but the argument is the same.

The finding claims the Gospel of Mary was preserved in 'Syriac, Georgian, Armenian, Coptic, Ethiopic' — but the speaker is actually describing the Acts of Paul and Thecla, not the Gospel of Mary. The Gospel of Mary survives only in Coptic and Greek fragments. Regardless, the textual transmission pattern of a non-canonical text across languages does not bear on Chapter 4's arguments about Johannine authorship, dating, or reliability. It is general early Christian textual history, not specific evidence for any argument the book makes.

Chapter 4 inventory documents Mary Magdalene as first resurrection witness and the argument that male editors obscured female testimony (John 21:24 'we who vouch'). Q&A documents 'women's memories don't count as much as men's memories.' The finding restates the same argument.

Chapter 4 inventory 'Archaeological evidence (lines 106-117)' and 'Topographical accuracy (lines 706-735)' extensively document John's detailed Palestinian geography including Jacob's Well, Siloam pool, Sheep gate pool. The finding restates the same evidence.

Chapter 4 discusses John's genre as Greco-Roman bios (lines 792-797) but does not cite Stibbe's specific argument that John is composed in the form of Greek tragedy specifically paralleling Euripides. The Greek tragedy genre classification is a more specific genre claim than bios, providing new evidence for the existing argument about John's Greek literary form.

This finding about Artemis's perpetual virginity influencing Christian Marian theology belongs in Chapter 3 (Greco-Christian framework, divine titles, theological parallels) rather than Chapter 4's focus on Gospel reliability and Johannine authorship. While John's traditional association with Ephesus is mentioned, the Artemis-Mary theological connection is Chapter 3 material about how Greek divine feminine attributes were absorbed into Christian theology.

Chapter 4 inventory 'John leaves out the Eucharist (lines 678-705)' documents water-to-wine as Dionysus's signature act. The finding about McDonald's research on Dionysian traditions and water-to-wine is the same argument.

Chapter 4 documents Greek literary conventions throughout and the chapter's genre analysis (lines 792-797) establishes Gospel authors worked within Hellenistic literary conventions. The finding about NT borrowing from Homer, Euripides, and mystery poets is the same general argument.

Chapter 4 notes John's multiple editions and Dionysian parallels but does not cite MacDonald's specific argument that the earliest recoverable layer portrays Jesus as a divine donor deity comparable to Dionysus, or that ancient Christian readers (Clement of Alexandria, medieval poets) explicitly recognized these parallels. The ancient reception evidence (Clement, medieval poets recognizing Dionysian Jesus) is new.

The three-stage model (Dionysian -> Anti-Jewish -> Beloved Disciple) is new evidence already noted. The additional claim that Johannine epistles were written BEFORE the gospel and quoted ~40 times within it is also not in the chapter inventory. Both are new evidence for the existing argument about John's compositional history.

This finding about Homeric parallels in Mark's resurrection narrative (guards at tomb paralleling Hermes conducting Priam past sleeping guards in Iliad 24) is about Mark's Gospel, not John. It belongs in Chapter 4's brief Mark section or more likely in a general Gospel analysis section, but the specific evidence pertains to Mark, not the Johannine material that is Chapter 4's primary focus.

Chapter 4 documents Mary Magdalene's prominence through textual evidence (canonical and apocryphal). The Talpiyot Tomb ossuary evidence (Mariamne inscription) is archaeological evidence not cited in the chapter inventory. This is new physical evidence for the existing argument about Mary Magdalene's special relationship to Jesus.
Chapter 4 extensively documents John's Logos theology as Hellenized presentation of Jesus throughout the Philo section and Prologue analysis. The finding restates the same argument.

Chapter 4 inventory 'John leaves out the Eucharist (lines 678-705)' documents Cana water-to-wine as Dionysus's signature act. The McDonald 'Dionysian gospel' label has been noted in earlier findings. The finding restates covered arguments.
Chapter 4 documents John-Dionysus parallels (Cana, vine symbolism). The finding restates MacDonald's mimesis criticism of John with the same core argument.

Chapter 4 inventory 'John leaves out the Eucharist (lines 678-705)' documents John's distinctive treatment (foot-washing instead, Bread of Life discourse in John 6). However, the specific contrast between John's soteriological mystical union framing ('you get salvation through this event') vs Paul/Mark's memorial framing is not explicitly articulated in the chapter. This theological-functional distinction is new evidence for the existing argument about John's distinctive Eucharistic theology.
Chapter 4 inventory documents Cana-Dionysus parallels extensively. The finding repeats the same argument.

This finding about Luke editing Mark's passion narrative into a Socratic hero's death is about Luke and Mark, not John. While it supports the general Greco-Christian framework, it belongs in the chapter sections discussing Synoptic Gospels more broadly, or Chapter 3. The evidence does not address Chapter 4's primary arguments about Johannine authorship and reliability.

Chapter 4 Q&A's female authorship Test 9 discusses educated upper-class women, and the chapter's argument for sophisticated authorship (Logos theology, archaeological knowledge, Greek literary competence) implies elite authorship. The finding restates the same argument.
Chapter 4 inventory 'Identity of the Beloved Disciple (lines 94-151)' cites the same texts. Q&A documents the cross-textual love language matrix. Same evidence.
Chapter 4 inventory cites Gospel of Mary (Coptic 17-18) with Peter's complaint. Q&A documents this extensively. Same evidence.
Chapter 4 documents John-Dionysus parallels. The finding is a general reference to McDonald's work, restating covered arguments.
Chapter 4 documents John's sophisticated composition and genre. The finding about gospel writers as educated elites restates the same argument.

This finding about Isis-like women caretakers of Osiris paralleling women at Jesus's death belongs in Chapter 3 (Greco-Christian divine parallels, Egyptian theological connections) or Chapter 5 (Egyptian theology section, which already documents John-Egyptian parallels). It does not address Chapter 4's specific arguments about Johannine authorship or dating.

This finding about Gospel of Thomas's gender transformation saying anchored in Egyptian funerary practices belongs in Chapter 3 (Egyptian theological parallels) rather than Chapter 4's focus on Gospel reliability and Johannine authorship. It concerns Egyptian cultural practices and Gospel of Thomas theology, not Johannine material.

Chapter 4 inventory 'Dating of the Gospel of John (lines 34-70)' argues for early Johannine dating using P52 and attestation order. However, Litwa's specific chronology (John c.100, prologue c.120, canonical Luke c.150) with the distinction between the gospel body and prologue dating, and the argument that John predates canonical Luke, provides a new scholarly dating framework supporting the existing Johannine primacy argument.

Chapter 4 inventory 'Gospel of Marcion (lines 1278-1309)' documents Marcion's gospel as possibly earlier edition of Luke, with birth narratives as secondary additions. The finding covers the same argument about Luke's textual modification.

Chapter 4 discusses John's textual transmission (lines 455-479) but does not identify the spearing passage in John 19:34 as a probable interpolation designed to suppress claims Jesus survived crucifixion. This specific textual-critical identification is new evidence for the existing argument about Johannine textual transmission and editorial revision.

Chapter 4 inventory 'Argument from Bodily Silence (lines 614-677)' addresses Greek cultural conventions for the Beloved Disciple relationship. The finding restates the same argument about John using Greek relationship paradigms for a Greek audience.

Chapter 4 documents Mary Magdalene's prominence but does not include Crossan's argument that Magdala was a major fishing village and Mary was likely a prominent fishing business owner whose enterprise was damaged by Antipas's commercialization. Her economic status as the basis for her prominence is new evidence for the existing argument about Mary's social standing.

Chapter 4 inventory 'Philo of Alexandria (lines 755-790)' and Chapter 3 Logos section cover the philosophical meaning of logos. The finding restates the same argument with a slightly different English gloss.

Chapter 4 documents Cana-Dionysus and vine symbolism. However, the extensive list of specific parallels — divine disguise, willing arrest, the Pentheus-Jesus parallel in the passion, the defiled royal corpse raised in a garden — goes well beyond what is enumerated in the chapter inventory. These specific Bacchae narrative parallels are new evidence for the existing Dionysian argument.

Same as 1TGmi4JZapk_1. The Eurocleia-anointing-woman Homeric parallel with the 'far and wide' wordplay is not in the chapter inventory. New evidence for the existing argument about Greek literary conventions in the Gospels.

Chapter 4 inventory 'Luke as Greek historical writing (lines 1320-1363)' explicitly documents 'Luke 1:1-4 opening mirrors Greek historical writing formula (Herodotus, Thucydides).' The finding restates the same argument.

Chapter 4 discusses John's Greco-Roman literary context but does not cite the Plato-Socrates comparison as a framework for understanding the evangelist's literary method. The specific analogy (evangelist has freedom to use Jesus to express own theology, as Plato used Socrates) is new framing for the existing argument about Greek literary conventions in John.
Chapter 4 inventory 'Dating of the Gospel of John (lines 72-92)' explicitly states 'John 21 is by a different author, an appendix.' The finding restates the same argument.
Chapter 4 documents Greek literary conventions in Gospel composition throughout. The finding's general claim about Gospels imitating Greek poetry is the same argument.

Chapter 4 documents Cana-Dionysus parallels but does not cite the specific January 6 date connection — that Dionysus's wine miracle was celebrated on January 6, the same date Christians later commemorated Epiphany. The calendrical correspondence is new evidence for the existing argument.
Same as rM_TG98nslU_0. The specific prologue-to-Bacchae-monologue parallel is not in the chapter inventory. New evidence for the existing argument.

Chapter 4 documents Cana-Dionysus parallels. However, the specific identification of 'grace upon grace' as 'gift upon gift' making Jesus a gift-giving deity, with Dionysus's epithet Ploutos Phora ('wealthbringer') as the direct parallel, is not in the chapter inventory. The specific Greek terminology connection is new evidence.

Neither the chapter inventory nor Q&A argues that Mary Magdalene's 'seven demons' should be reinterpreted through the lens of Maenad ecstatic possession by Dionysus — spiritual ecstasy mistaken for/described as madness. This is a new argument (reinterpretation of 'seven demons' through Dionysian framework) with new evidence (Maenad possession descriptions).

Chapter 4 documents John-Dionysus parallels. The specific framing of John as a 'supplement' to the synoptics designed to compete in the 'religious marketplace' against Dionysian religion — offering Jesus as a donor deity who also gives gifts — adds a specific compositional-purpose argument not in the chapter.
Chapter 4 inventory 'John leaves out the Eucharist (lines 678-705)' documents the Dionysian connection to eucharistic theology (wine, flesh, Dionysus). The finding restates the same argument.
Chapter 4 inventory 'Introduction (lines 13-31)' states 'Gospel of Thomas likely predating canonical gospels.' The finding restates the same claim.

Chapter 4 documents John's multiple editions and John 21 as appendix. However, the specific claim that the Thomas touching scene may be an interpolation (not just chapter 21) is not in the chapter inventory. This specific interpolation claim is new evidence for the existing argument about Johannine editorial revision.

Chapter 4 inventory 'Gendered Focalization / Song of Songs (lines 270-325)' documents John 20 recognition scene. The 'don't touch me' (noli me tangere) and Mary as first witness are part of the existing treatment.

Chapter 4 inventory mentions Gospel of Thomas predating canonical gospels but does not argue that John was specifically written as a polemic against Thomas's teachings about spiritual equality with Jesus. This compositional purpose (anti-Thomas polemic) is new evidence for the existing argument about John's compositional context.

Chapter 4 documents John-Dionysus parallels but does not cite Nonas, the 5th-century Egyptian poet and Dionysus believer who wrote a paraphrase of John's Gospel with Jesus bearing Dionysian traits. This ancient reception evidence is new evidence for the existing Dionysian argument.
Chapter 4 documents Cana-Dionysus parallels and Song of Songs recognition scene parallels. The finding combines two covered topics without adding new specific evidence.
Chapter 4 documents John-Dionysus parallels extensively. The McDonald thesis about John imitating Euripides Bacchae has been covered in multiple earlier findings.
Chapter 4 inventory 'Philo of Alexandria (lines 755-790)' documents the John-Philo connection with extensive specific citations. The finding restates this.

Chapter 4 documents John-Dionysus parallels and the Greco-Christian audience framework. The finding about John competing with Dionysian mystery religion in Greek communities restates covered arguments.

Chapter 4 inventory 'Introduction (lines 13-31)' discusses apocryphal texts being dismissed as late but some being demonstrably early. The existence of many gospels beyond the canonical four is implicit context. No new evidence for Chapter 4's specific arguments.
Chapter 4 documents John-Dionysus parallels extensively. The finding about John's mimesis of Bacchae has been covered in multiple earlier findings.

Chapter 4 inventory 'Topographical accuracy (lines 706-735)' documents 'Aramaic words retained: Rabbouni, Messias, Golgotha — raw transliterations for Greek audience.' The finding about John explaining Hebrew terms for a non-Jewish audience is the same evidence.

Chapter 4 inventory 'Miracles in John (lines 837-876)' lists the seven signs but does not cite the parallel seven 'I am' sayings and seven witnesses declaring Jesus Son of God as a deliberate tripartite numerical structure. The systematic sevenfold pattern across three domains (signs, sayings, witnesses) is new evidence for the existing argument about John's sophisticated literary composition.

Same as cjCRhgD_XIQ_4 and kbLm_Xiqih8_4. The synagogue-expulsion as institutional context for John is not in the chapter inventory. New evidence for existing argument about John's institutional context.
Chapter 4 extensively documents John's Greek audience and Logos theology. The specific mention of 'idria' (Greek ritual vessels) connects to the Cana narrative already covered in the chapter.

The finding contains two elements: (1) the Dionysian Gospel as earliest layer supports the book's Greco-Christian framework, but (2) MacDonald's argument that 'The Gospel of John, in its earliest manifestation, knows the Synoptic Gospels because the same Homeric imitations appear' directly contradicts the book's thesis of Johannine primacy and independent eyewitness status. The book argues John was written first by an eyewitness; MacDonald argues John was a deliberate supplement to the synoptics.
Chapter 4 documents John-Dionysus/Bacchae parallels. The finding restates the MacDonald thesis already covered in multiple earlier findings.

This finding about Homeric parallels in Acts (Gamaliel episode) and Mark (disciples as Odysseus's crew) pertains to those books specifically, not to John. It belongs in the chapter sections discussing Synoptics and Acts more broadly.

Chapter 4 inventory discusses the Samaritan woman as narrative hinge in the 'Gendered Structures' section (John 4, ~20 verses sustained dialogue, first public evangelist). However, the political-allegorical reading of five husbands as five empires (Egypt, Assyria, Medo-Persia, Babylon, Greece) with Rome as the non-husband sixth is not in the chapter. This is new exegetical evidence for the existing argument about John's sophisticated theological symbolism.

Neither the chapter inventory nor Q&A discusses the Samaritan woman as potentially incorporating the Helena/fallen Sophia figure from Greek Gnostic mythology (associated with Simon Magus and Dositheus). This is both a new argument (Samaritan woman as Gnostic Sophia figure) and new evidence (the Simon/Helena tradition) not present in the book. Price himself notes this is speculative.

The vesica Pisces as a mathematical-geometric form underlying the Christian fish symbol does not bear on any argument in Chapter 4 about Johannine authorship, dating, reliability, or even the broader Gospel analysis sections. It is interesting trivia about Christian iconography but does not address any argument in any chapter of the book.

Chapter 4 documents John-Dionysus parallels but does not cite the Lazarus narrative's parallels to Euripides' Alcestis — 12 distinct motifs including hospitality, leisurely pace, warnings from relatives, face-wrapping, three-day motifs, deification foreshadowing. The specific Alcestis parallel with 12 enumerated motifs is new evidence for the existing argument about John's Greek literary dependence.
Chapter 4 documents John-Dionysus/Bacchae parallels. The finding restates the same argument.
Chapter 4 inventory 'Gospel of Marcion (lines 1278-1309)' documents this exact argument about Marcion preserving an earlier Luke edition without birth narrative and genealogy.

The entire methodology of Chapter 4 is to understand the Gospels in their original contexts (Greco-Christian framework) rather than accepting later canonical interpretive overlays. This is the chapter's foundational approach, not a new argument.

Chapter 4 inventory 'Dating of the Gospel of John (lines 34-70)' states 'Irenaeus attests Mark/Matthew/Luke only in 180 AD.' The finding about titles as later additions and anonymous original authorship is the same argument.

Chapter 4's entire project of challenging traditional authorship (John son of Zebedee) and proposing Mary Magdalene implicitly frames traditional attributions as unreliable. The finding restates this framing.
Chapter 4 documents John-Dionysus parallels extensively. The finding restates the MacDonald thesis already covered in many earlier findings.

The book argues the Beloved Disciple was a real person (Mary Magdalene) and a genuine eyewitness. Dr. Hugo Mendes's argument that the Beloved Disciple is a 'fictional literary device inserted to confer false eyewitness authority' directly contradicts the book's central Chapter 4 thesis about real eyewitness authorship.

This finding about Mark's Gospel imitating Homer through Greek literary synchresis pertains to Mark's composition, not John. It supports the general Greco-Christian framework but belongs in a Mark-focused discussion, not Chapter 4's primary focus on Johannine material.

Same as 1TGmi4JZapk_1 and JOsGRR-vqUw_2. The Casper Larson Homeric recognition scene scholarship identifying John's anointing scene as paralleling Odyssey recognition is not in the chapter inventory. New evidence for the existing argument.
Same Homeric parallel as above. Not in chapter inventory. New evidence for existing argument about Greek literary conventions in John.
Chapter 4 extensively documents John's Greek audience and Logos cosmology. The finding restates covered arguments.

This finding about Mary Magdalene's Herodian connections and financial support of Jesus's movement pertains to the historical reconstruction of Jesus's social context, which belongs in Chapter 2 (Jesus's background, social world) rather than Chapter 4's focus on Gospel reliability and Johannine authorship. Chapter 2 inventory discusses Herodian connections.

Neither the chapter inventory nor Q&A discusses MacDonald's argument that 'Magdalene' (tower town) places Mary in the Andromache archetype from Homer — watching her husband die from a tower, paralleling Andromache watching Hector die from Troy's tower. This is a new argument (Mary as Homeric Andromache figure) with new evidence (the etymological/narrative parallel).

Chapter 4 inventory 'Identity of the Beloved Disciple (lines 94-151)' and Q&A 'Redaction Oddities: Masked Woman Hypothesis' both discuss John 19:25-27 and the Beloved Disciple's special relationship. Same evidence.
Chapter 4 documents John-Dionysus/Bacchae parallels extensively. The finding restates the same argument.

Chapter 4 documents Mary Magdalene's prominence from Gospel of Mary. However, the specific argument that the Gospel of Mary's 'seven powers' were deliberately transformed into Luke's 'seven demons' to discredit Mary is not in the chapter inventory. This intertextual discrediting mechanism is new evidence for the existing argument about systematic suppression of Mary's authority.

Chapter 4 inventory 'Dating of the Gospel of John (lines 72-92)' explicitly states 'John 21 is by a different author, an appendix; John 1-20 ends naturally at 20:30-31.' The finding restates the same argument.

The specific three-stage compositional model (Dionysian -> anti-Jewish -> beloved disciple) has been noted in earlier findings as new evidence. This finding adds the specific framing of Jesus as a 'new Dionysos' at the compositional foundation. Same new evidence as V6shchstY9k_2 and -yLHxWIezdA_1.

Chapter 4 documents John's Greek audience throughout. However, the specific geographic identification of Western Asia Minor with its Dionysus cult sites, and the mention of Hellenistic Jews as part of the audience who would recognize Dionysian parallels, adds geographic specificity not in the chapter inventory. New evidence for the existing argument.
